explanation: An Ethernet (MAC) address is 48 bits: the first 24 are the OUI assigned by the IEEE to a specific vendor, and the last 24 are assigned by that vendor to the card. That two-level allocation is what makes the address globally unique, not merely unique on the local segment.

stem: Ripple adder Vs carry look ahead
explanation: A ripple-carry adder must wait for the carry to propagate through each full adder in turn, so its delay grows linearly with the number of bits (O(n)). A carry-look-ahead adder computes all the carries in parallel from generate and propagate terms, so its delay is roughly logarithmic, making it faster.

stem: Safest way to connect to a secure network through public network is
explanation: Answer unverified: the keyed answer is TCP, but neither TCP nor UDP provides security, so as printed the question has no truly correct option. The real answer to 'connect securely across a public network' is a VPN or an encrypted tunnel; the option list appears truncated. TCP is only the least-wrong pick because it is connection-oriented and is the transport that TLS runs over.

stem: For a database to grow bigger to include more data it needs
explanation: Scalability is the ability of a system to keep performing as its data volume or load grows, which is exactly what the stem describes. Extensibility is the distractor: it means the ability to add new features or new schema capability, not the ability to hold more data.
A terabyte is a unit of data size equal to 10^12 bytes, that is one trillion bytes (1000 GB). 'Flash' and 'storage' name a medium or a category, not a quantity, so they cannot answer 'a terabyte is'.

stem: a 32-bit bus when going to a memory it brings?
explanation: The data bus width is how many bits move per memory transfer: 32 bits / 8 bits per byte = 4 bytes. Do not confuse the data bus width with the address bus width, which sets how much memory can be addressed, not how much is fetched per cycle.

stem: Connected host in network with most redundancy?
explanation: In a full mesh every host has a direct link to every other host, so any single link or node failure still leaves multiple alternative paths, giving maximum redundancy. A ring gives each node only two paths and a star gives only one, through the central hub, which is a single point of failure.

stem: if you want to buy a software for your computer, you buy it depending on?

options.label: B
options.text: compatible with software’s you want to use

explanation: The derived answer is A (compatibility with hardware); the source document carries no key for this item. Whether purchased software will run at all is set by the host platform, that is the CPU, OS, RAM, and disk in the system requirements, so hardware compatibility is the binding constraint. 'Expensive' is not a correctness criterion, and option D is the classic compound trap that pairs a plausible clause with a bad one.

stem: why is IP used that ether net address?
explanation: IP addresses are hierarchical: the address splits into a network prefix plus a host part, so routers can aggregate many hosts under one prefix and forward on the prefix alone. Ethernet/MAC addresses are flat (vendor OUI plus serial number, no location information), so they cannot be summarized and are only usable inside one broadcast domain.

stem: The receive equalizer reduces delay distortions using a
explanation: A receive equalizer is a transversal filter built from a tapped delay line: it takes copies of the signal delayed by successive tap intervals, weights them and sums them so the echoes cancel the intersymbol interference caused by delay distortion. The distractors are unrelated modem parts: a gearshift is speed fallback, a descrambler undoes scrambling, and a difference engine is a mechanical calculator.

options.label: B
options.text: The papers required to be brought is a given page request

options.label: C
options.text: The movement of pages in and out of the memory

options.label: D
options.text: The number of pages of executing programs loaded in memory

explanation: Page traffic is the rate at which pages are moved in and out of main memory (page-ins and page-outs), which is why excessive page traffic is the symptom of thrashing. Option D describes the resident set (how many pages are currently loaded), a static count, not the movement.

stem: Which of the following is not a fundamental process state?
explanation: Answer unverified: the source key marks D (Blocked), but Blocked/waiting IS one of the fundamental process states (new, ready, running, blocked, terminated) and the keyed option even concedes it by adding 'can be substituted with wait'. The genuine odd one out is B, 'Executed', because the actual state is 'running/executing'; review panel derived B as the correct answer.

stem: LRU page replacement policy stands for
explanation: LRU stands for Least Recently Used: it evicts the page whose most recent reference is furthest in the past, using locality of reference as a proxy for which page will be needed last. The other three expansions are invented terms.

stem: Another name for IEEE 802.11 a is
explanation: The whole IEEE 802.11 WLAN family, including 802.11a, is branded Wi-Fi. Ethernet is 802.3 and WiMAX is 802.16. The trap is D: 802.11a is a different PHY from 802.11g (5 GHz versus 2.4 GHz, though both reach 54 Mbps), so they are not other names for each other.

stem: The kernel of the operating system remains in primary memory because

options.label: B
options.text: It is mostly used or called

explanation: Answer unverified: the source PDF carried no key for this item and only two options survived; the panel derived B. The kernel is kept memory-resident because it is the most frequently called code in the system, servicing every system call, interrupt and context switch, so paging it out would fault on nearly every dispatch. Managing interrupt calls (A) is something the kernel does, not the reason it must stay in primary memory.
